titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.orgName: University of South Carolina Provost Humanities Grants
University of South Carolina Department of English
Center for Digital Humanities at the University of South Carolina
Magellan Scholar Program at the University of South Carolina
National Society of the Daughters of the American Revolution
Carnegie-Whitney Grant from the American Library Association

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

div.p: THE lonely wind-swept island of San Nicolas, eighty miles off the coast of Ventura County, California, is known as the "Isle of Skulls." A most peculiar name, but judging from recent reports of archæologists, who are now busy gathering the relics of a strange extinct race, this island is most appropriately called. San Nicolas and its erstwhile inhabitants have a history so curious that it is difficult of comprehension.

In 1835 the padres of Santa Barbara, learning that there were but sixteen persons left of the strange Indian race once living there, determined to rescue them from the island. They went over in a sloop, and succeeded, as they thought, in getting all on board. At the last moment an Indian woman returned for her child, and one of the frequent storms of the channel islands springing up, the sloop was driven away without her. The sloop went on the rocks of Point Conception, and all on board were lost.

p.span: Sixteen years later Capt. George Nidever and two men went from the coast on a sloop to hunt otter off San Nicolas. On landing they were, like Crusoe, astonished to discover human footprints in the sand. They saw no one, however, and a storm compelled them to put to sea.

It was two years thereafter that the adventurous captain, revolving in his mind the sight of the footprints in the uncanny island, determined to go and discover, and bring over the lonely woman of whom he had vaguely heard. Men accompanied him, and at length they saw on the surf-beaten shore a woman with long tawny hair dressed in a queer garb of colored bird-skins, and scraping, with a bone knife, the blubber from a seal.

When suddenly surrounded and confronted she did not appear in the least afraid, but smiled, and then falling on her knees prayed to the sun. When by signs she was made to understand, and that she was to accompany her captors, she made no resistance.

div.p: They reached Santa Barbara across the rough sea, and the first thing the Indian woman saw was Dr. S. L. Shaw, now eighty years old and yet living there, riding a horse. She had never seen nor heard of any object like it, and thought the man and horse were one, and she knelt on the shore and offered her devotions to it. Two weeks afterward the last inhabitant of rock-ribbed, tempest-tossed San Nicolas died from eating food to which she was unaccustomed, furnished by her rescuers, and the curtain fell on her race forever. Had the woman rescued, then fifty-two years old, not died, a story passing strange would no doubt have been told of the race so long hemmed in by the waste of waters about. As it is, much of the story is shrouded in mystery forever. Her dress of red and blue feathers, a wonderful creation of barbaric hands, was sent to the Pope at Rome.

p.span: As far back as the memory of man runs, hundreds of white skeletons have dotted the valleys and hill-sides, and strange utensils of serpentine[,] sandstone and steatite are constantly being found there among the human bones. Relic hunters have gathered mortars, pestles, ollas, toy steatite canoes, and other curious things there for some years past, and shipped them to various Eastern and European universities.
